learning_objectives: Define serious games and distinguish them from entertainment games
Trace the historical evolution of serious games from military simulations to modern applications
Identify the major domains where serious games are deployed
readings: Serious Games: Mechanisms and Effects, Ch. 1-2 (Ritterfeld, Cody, Vorderer)

lecture_notes.introduction: Serious games apply game design principles to solve real-world problems. This course explores how interactive experiences can educate, train, persuade, and heal.
key_concepts: Definition and taxonomy of serious games
Historical roots in military war games and flight simulators
Domains: education, health, defense, corporate training, social impact
The tension between entertainment value and instructional effectiveness
Measuring outcomes beyond fun
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analysis of three landmark serious games: America's Army (recruitment), Re-Mission (pediatric cancer), and Foldit (protein folding)
setup_requirements: Access to course LMS for discussion forums
Steam or itch.io account for game analysis assignments

review.value: Games designed with a primary purpose beyond entertainment, using game mechanics to achieve learning, behavioral, or social objectives
Balancing intrinsic motivation from gameplay with extrinsic instructional goals without undermining either

learning_objectives: Distinguish gamification from serious game design
Apply the Octalysis framework to analyze gamified systems
Explain how self-determination theory underpins effective gamification
readings: Deci & Ryan, Self-Determination Theory overview paper

lecture_notes.introduction: Gamification adds game elements to non-game contexts. Understanding the psychology behind why these elements work is essential to designing systems that motivate rather than manipulate.
key_concepts: Gamification vs. game-based learning vs. serious games
Octalysis framework: eight core drives of motivation
Self-determination theory: autonomy, competence, relatedness
Intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation and the overjustification effect
Points, badges, leaderboards (PBL) and their limitations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Octalysis analysis of Duolingo's gamification system, mapping each feature to a core drive
setup_requirements: Access to Duolingo free tier for hands-on exploration

review.value: Epic Meaning, Accomplishment, Empowerment, Ownership, Social Influence, Scarcity, Unpredictability, Avoidance
External rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation when they replace internal satisfaction as the reason for engagement

learning_objectives: Apply Bloom's taxonomy to educational game design objectives
Design game mechanics that scaffold learning progressions
Evaluate educational games using evidence-based criteria
readings: Digital Games and Learning, Ch. 3-4 (Perrotta et al.)
Gee, What Video Games Have to Teach Us About Learning and Literacy, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Educational games are most effective when learning objectives and game mechanics reinforce each other. We examine frameworks for aligning pedagogy with play.
key_concepts: Bloom's taxonomy mapped to game mechanics
Zone of proximal development and adaptive difficulty
Constructivist learning through exploration and experimentation
Assessment embedded in gameplay vs. bolted-on quizzes
Transfer of learning from game context to real-world application
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparative analysis of DragonBox (algebra) and Kerbal Space Program (orbital mechanics) as educational games
setup_requirements: Access to at least one educational game for hands-on evaluation
Learning objective mapping template (provided on LMS)

review.value: Measuring player learning through gameplay actions and decisions rather than explicit test questions
Gradually increasing challenge aligned with Vygotsky's zone of proximal development to keep players in a flow state while learning

learning_objectives: Identify therapeutic goals addressable through game-based interventions
Analyze the design principles behind FDA-approved digital therapeutics
Evaluate clinical evidence for health game effectiveness
readings: Games for Health Journal, selected papers on Re-Mission 2 and EndeavorRx
Thompson et al., Serious Video Games for Health, review article

lecture_notes.introduction: Health games represent one of the most rigorously validated categories of serious games. From physical rehabilitation to mental health, games are being prescribed alongside traditional medicine.
key_concepts: Categories: physical therapy, cognitive rehabilitation, mental health, chronic disease management
FDA pathway for digital therapeutics (EndeavorRx as case study)
Clinical trial design for validating game-based interventions
Patient adherence and engagement over time
Ethical considerations in health game design
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design review of EndeavorRx (FDA-approved ADHD treatment game), examining how specific mechanics target attention control
setup_requirements: Access to published clinical trial data (provided via LMS)

review.value: Software-based interventions that deliver evidence-based therapeutic treatments, reviewed and approved through regulatory pathways like the FDA
Game mechanics specifically designed to encourage sustained engagement with therapeutic content over weeks or months of treatment

learning_objectives: Design simulation-based training that transfers to real-world performance
Apply engagement metrics to measure gamification ROI in corporate settings
Identify common failure modes in corporate gamification initiatives
readings: The Gamification of Learning and Instruction, Ch. 5-7 (Kapp)
Harvard Business Review, 'Gamification at Work' case studies

lecture_notes.introduction: Corporate training and simulation represent the largest market segment for serious games. Success requires clear performance metrics and demonstrated return on investment.
key_concepts: Simulation fidelity spectrum: low-fidelity scenarios to full virtual environments
Kirkpatrick's four levels of training evaluation applied to games
Engagement metrics: completion rates, time-on-task, voluntary replay
Common gamification anti-patterns: pointsification, chocolate-covered broccoli
Case studies: Walmart VR safety training, Deloitte Leadership Academy
lecture_notes.demonstration: ROI analysis of a corporate gamification program using Kirkpatrick's model, comparing traditional e-learning to game-based training outcomes
setup_requirements: Access to sample training effectiveness data (provided on LMS)

review.value: Reaction (did they like it), Learning (did they learn), Behavior (did they apply it), Results (did it impact the organization)
The superficial addition of points and badges without meaningful game design, resulting in short-term engagement spikes followed by rapid decline

learning_objectives: Design games that drive measurable social change
Apply accessibility guidelines to ensure inclusive serious game design
Evaluate the ethical implications of persuasive game design

lecture_notes.introduction: Games can shift perspectives, build empathy, and catalyze social action. This session examines games designed for social impact and the responsibility that comes with persuasive design.
key_concepts: Procedural rhetoric: making arguments through game mechanics
Empathy games and perspective-taking design
Accessibility standards: WCAG applied to games, motor/visual/cognitive accommodations
Measuring social impact: attitude change, behavioral intention, real-world action
Ethics of persuasive design and manipulation boundaries
lecture_notes.demonstration: Play and analysis of Papers, Please (immigration ethics) and Spent (poverty simulation), examining how mechanics create persuasive arguments
setup_requirements: Access to Papers, Please and/or Spent (browser-based)

review.value: The practice of making arguments through the rules and mechanics of a game rather than through narrative or visual content alone
Building games accessible to players with diverse abilities from the start, including remappable controls, subtitle options, colorblind modes, and difficulty adjustments

quizzes.topic: Serious Games Foundations and Gamification Theory

questions.question: A company implements a leaderboard for its sales team, and after an initial spike in performance, engagement drops significantly within two months. Using self-determination theory, explain why this happened and propose a redesign that addresses the underlying motivational issue.
questions.answer: The leaderboard relies on extrinsic motivation (competition and social comparison) which can undermine intrinsic motivation through the overjustification effect. It threatens autonomy (forced participation), reduces competence feelings for those consistently at the bottom, and can damage relatedness through toxic competition. A redesign should incorporate personal progress tracking (competence), optional participation and multiple paths to recognition (autonomy), and team-based collaborative challenges (relatedness).

rubric.key: Identifies overjustification or extrinsic motivation problem
Correctly applies at least two SDT needs (autonomy, competence, relatedness)
Proposes concrete redesign addressing identified issues

questions.question: Explain the difference between a serious game and gamification, and give one specific example of each applied to the same domain (e.g., healthcare, education, or corporate training). For each example, identify which Octalysis core drive is most leveraged.
questions.answer: A serious game is a complete game designed with a primary purpose beyond entertainment, while gamification adds game elements to an existing non-game system. In healthcare: Re-Mission (serious game) is a full action game where players fight cancer cells, primarily leveraging Core Drive 2 (Accomplishment) through meaningful victories. A hospital patient portal with streaks for medication adherence (gamification) adds game elements to an existing system, primarily leveraging Core Drive 6 (Scarcity/Impatience) through streak maintenance pressure. The key distinction is that one is a standalone game experience, while the other layers game mechanics onto an existing activity.

rubric.key: Clear and accurate distinction between serious games and gamification
Valid examples from the same domain with sufficient detail
Correctly identifies and justifies Octalysis core drives for each

quizzes.topic: Applied Serious Game Design and Evaluation

questions.question: A hospital wants to create a game to help children aged 8-12 manage Type 1 diabetes. Using Kirkpatrick's four levels of evaluation, describe what you would measure at each level to determine if the game is effective. Include at least one specific metric per level.
questions.answer: Level 1 (Reaction): Player satisfaction surveys and session duration/voluntary replay rates measuring whether children enjoy playing. Level 2 (Learning): Pre/post knowledge tests on carbohydrate counting, insulin timing, and symptom recognition demonstrating knowledge gain. Level 3 (Behavior): HbA1c monitoring logs and self-reported blood glucose checking frequency over 3 months showing real-world behavior change. Level 4 (Results): Reduction in emergency room visits for diabetic episodes and improvement in HbA1c levels over 6 months demonstrating clinical outcomes.

rubric.key: Correctly names and applies all four Kirkpatrick levels
Provides specific, measurable metrics for each level
Metrics are appropriate for the pediatric diabetes context

questions.question: Describe three specific accessibility features you would implement in a serious game designed to teach emergency evacuation procedures to corporate employees, and explain how each feature ensures the game can be used by people with different disabilities without reducing the training effectiveness.
questions.answer: 1) Multiple input modes: support keyboard-only navigation, switch controls, and voice commands alongside mouse/touch, ensuring employees with motor disabilities can complete all training scenarios. The evacuation route choices remain meaningful regardless of input method. 2) Audio descriptions and haptic feedback: provide narrated descriptions of visual hazards and vibration cues for proximity to dangers, enabling visually impaired employees to learn spatial awareness of evacuation routes through alternative sensory channels. 3) Adjustable cognitive load: offer simplified UI mode with reduced simultaneous information, extended time limits, and step-by-step guided mode alongside the standard timed simulation, allowing employees with cognitive disabilities to learn the same evacuation procedures at an appropriate pace.

rubric.key: Three distinct accessibility features addressing different disability types
Clear explanation of how each feature accommodates specific disabilities
Demonstrates that training effectiveness is preserved for each accommodation

major_assignments.title: Gamification System Design and Analysis
major_assignments.description: Select a real-world non-game context (workplace, education, health, civic engagement) and design a comprehensive gamification system. Conduct an Octalysis analysis of your design and compare it against an existing gamification implementation in the same domain.
objectives: Apply the Octalysis framework to design a gamification system grounded in behavioral psychology
Demonstrate understanding of intrinsic vs. extrinsic motivation in system design
Critically evaluate an existing gamification implementation using evidence-based criteria
Produce professional design documentation suitable for stakeholder presentation
deliverables: Gamification design document with complete Octalysis analysis (8-10 pages)
Comparative evaluation of an existing system in the same domain (3-4 pages)
User journey map showing player progression through the gamified system
Presentation slides summarizing the design rationale (10-15 slides)

criteria: Gamification mechanics are well-suited to the chosen domain
All eight Octalysis core drives are addressed with clear justification
Design avoids common anti-patterns (pointsification, exploitative mechanics)
User progression is well-paced and motivationally sustainable
Self-determination theory correctly applied to design decisions
Intrinsic and extrinsic motivation balanced appropriately
Behavioral psychology principles cited with academic sources
Potential overjustification effects identified and mitigated
Existing system evaluated using consistent criteria
Strengths and weaknesses identified with supporting evidence
Clear articulation of how proposed design improves on existing approaches
Fair and balanced assessment
Professional writing and document formatting
Clear visual diagrams and user journey map
Effective presentation slides with speaker notes
Proper citation of all sources
Comprehensive gamification design with strong theoretical foundation
Thorough Octalysis analysis demonstrating mastery of the framework
Insightful comparative evaluation with actionable conclusions
Professional presentation quality suitable for industry stakeholders

major_assignments.title: Serious Game Prototype
major_assignments.description: Design and develop a playable serious game prototype addressing a real-world problem in education, health, training, or social impact. The game must demonstrate measurable learning outcomes or behavioral objectives with embedded assessment.
objectives: Create a functional serious game prototype with clearly defined learning or behavioral objectives
Implement embedded assessment mechanics that measure player progress toward objectives
Apply accessibility guidelines to ensure inclusive design
Conduct playtesting and iterate based on evidence
deliverables: Playable prototype (digital or tabletop with full rules document)
Design document including target audience, objectives, and mechanics justification (6-8 pages)
Embedded assessment specification describing what is measured and how
Playtesting report with at least 5 participants and documented iterations

criteria: Clear alignment between game mechanics and learning/behavioral objectives
Embedded assessment meaningfully measures player progress
Game maintains engagement while delivering on its serious purpose
Target audience and context are well-defined and addressed
Prototype is functional and playable without critical bugs
Core mechanics are implemented and demonstrate the design intent
Assessment data is collected and accessible for analysis
Build runs on intended platform without errors
At least three accessibility features implemented and documented
Design considers diverse player abilities from the outset
Accommodations do not reduce effectiveness of the serious purpose
Audit checklist completed with honest assessment of gaps
Playtesting conducted with appropriate participants
Data collected systematically with clear methodology
Iterations documented with before/after comparisons
Reflection on findings and future improvements
Prototype effectively addresses a real-world problem through game design
Embedded assessment demonstrates measurable outcomes
Inclusive design with documented accessibility features
Evidence-based iteration from structured playtesting

quizzes.description: Assessment of theoretical knowledge in gamification frameworks and serious game design principles

gamification_design_project.description: Gamification system design with Octalysis analysis and comparative evaluation

serious_game_prototype.description: Playable serious game prototype with embedded assessment and accessibility features

participation_and_critiques.description: Active participation in game analysis discussions, peer playtesting, and design critiques

final_reflection_portfolio.description: Curated portfolio of course work with reflective analysis connecting theory to practice

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all playtesting sessions, peer critiques, and guest speaker events. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Playtesting deadlines are firm due to scheduled participant sessions and cannot be extended.
course_policies.collaboration: Individual assignments must be completed independently. The serious game prototype may be completed individually or in teams of up to 3, with peer evaluation required for team submissions.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All designs and written work must be original. External game assets must be properly licensed and attributed. Plagiarism of game mechanics documentation will be treated as academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to a game development tool of their choice (Unity, Godot, Twine, tabletop prototyping materials). Free options are acceptable. All playtesting sessions require a functional build or prototype.
